section: Specific changes to Political practice and rhetoric
content:
After looking at the Prefatory comments to the section on "Political practice and rhetoric", then it seems that it is apparently poorly written and seems to verge upon WP:Synth problems. The citations being used there appear chronologically scattered with only one mention of the time frame being discussed as "2016" in the prose followed by one mention for "2024" in the prose. The citations throughout the section, however, seem scattered over a disparate time frames ranging from 2016-2017, to 2020-2021, to 2024-2025 in no particular order; they also seem almost randomly sequenced. For example, the first cite in this Preface is from 2024, but then 2 sentences later the prose indicates the time frame is suddenly 2016. Combining historical contexts from these differing years as if they form a single insight appears to be against WP:Synth which speaks against Wikipedia editors combining separated historical contexts as if they were discussing the same time frame. Because of this issue with the Preface, it seems appropriate to ask if other editors agree that the WP:Synth issues should be corrected by trimming this preface, or, if the Prefatory sentences (6-7 sentences) should be removed. Should the Prefatory comments be removed as poorly written and unchronological, or, should the Prefatory comments (6-7 sentences) be trimmed and rewritten to correct the WP:Synth issues of different time frames being nonchronologically superimposed next to each other. [[User:ErnestKrause|ErnestKrause]] ([[User talk:ErnestKrause|talk]]) 16:28, 28 January 2026 (UTC)
:Not seeing any synth problems, no. Using sources from across a long period of time is normal; and the sources being summarized are largely not focused on specific historical events but, themselves, on broad summaries of Trump's political practice and rhetoric. If you think there's ''specific'' synthesis issues, I suggest focusing on individual sentences and being clear about the implication you think they're making, along with ...

section: Tasnim News as a source for Iranian claims
content: Can I use the Tasnim News Agency as a source for the Iranian claims of US casualties, even though it is not a reliable source? For example, the following article states that the United States suffered 650 casualties: https://www.tasnimnews.ir/en/news/2026/03/03/3530082/us-forces-suffer-650-casualties-as-iranian-strikes-force-retreat-of-american-carrier-irgc [[User:Balthasar144|Balthasar144]] ([[User talk:Balthasar144|talk]]) 13:53, 3 March 2026 (UTC) :I feel like you could, but you'd have to format it like: :'''Iranian claim''': :Biased # [[User:JaxsonR|JaxsonR]] ([[User talk:JaxsonR|talk]]) 14:37, 3 March 2026 (UTC) ::Adding ''"Biased"''? This will be very unencyclopedic. ''"Iranian claim:"'' is enough, or may be accompanied by an inline note if necessary. [[User:Ahammed Saad|Ahammed Saad]] ([[User talk:Ahammed Saad|talk]]) 15:05, 3 March 2026 (UTC) :::"Claim" should also be avoided in most cases ([[MOS:CLAIM]]). '''[[User:Thebiguglyalien|Thebiguglyalien]]''' ([[User talk:Thebiguglyalien|talk]]) 17:00, 3 March 2026 (UTC) :Why was it then removed? While Tasnim News may be an unreliable source for general news, it is still the semi-official news website of the IRGC. I think that, when it comes to what Iran or its military claims to have done, it can be used as a source. Like the state news agency IRNA, it is subject to state control, which means that what Tasnim claims is also the opinion of the government. So if you use it as the source and write „Per Iran“, I think that it follows the Wikipedia guidelines. [[User:Balthasar144|Balthasar144]] ([[User talk:Balthasar144|talk]]) 20:39, 3 March 2026 (UTC) :If it is not a [[Wikipedia:Reliable sources|reliable source]], you cannot use it to validate [[MOS:CLAIM|unconfirmed claims]]. The Africa Center for Strategic Studies, a think tank part of the U.S. Deptartment of Defense states the Houthis have ties with al-Shabaab, an al-Qaeda affiliate in Somalia [[Houthis#cite note-61|here]]. (the citation link has an extra l, which causes the 404 error)

Personally, I think this is highly questionable, ACSS bases their claim on a [https://docs.un.org/en/S/2025/71/Rev.1 2025 UN report], which in paragraph no. 40 states:

"Al-Shabaab was observed to have a developing relationship with the Houthis that was described as transactional or opportunistic, and not ideological. One Member State reported that Al-Shabaab held at least two meetings in Somalia with Houthi representatives in July and September, in which they requested advanced weapons and training."

Here are some important points:

- Only one unnamed member state made this claim

- Even the report describes the relationship as "transactional or opportunistic" and "not ideological", which weakens the case for real cooperation.

- A lack of evidence was presented in the source.

I think it's best to add "(alleged)" next to al-Shabaab in the non-state allies list per [[Wikipedia:Neutral point of view|WP:NPOV]]. The only source for the article's claim is a [https://africacenter.org/spotlight/al-shabaab-houthi-security-red-sea/ ACSS Spotlight article], citing a 2025 UN report citing one anonymous member state, and the relationship is described as “transactional or opportunistic.” [[User:Josemite|Josemite]] ([[User talk:Josemite|talk]]) 09:50, 1 August 2025 (UTC)

article: 2026 United States military buildup in the Middle East
content:
I've reverted to the 01:59 version of the article. This isn't a dispute over sourcing, the citations added are generally fine. The issues are:
Prose: The added content has frequent grammatical errors and awkward phrasing that make the article harder to read. Wikipedia's manual of style asks for clear, neutral, encyclopedic writing.
Length: At 90,000+ bytes this article is already long. Adding quotes and commentary from every analyst, cable news commentator, and interview subject who has weighed in on the situation inflates it further without improving coverage. Per [[WP:UNDUE]], not every perspective needs representation, the article should focus on primary actors and decisions, not a running log of reactions.
My suggestion would be to prioritize direct statements from government officials, military commanders, and negotiators over secondary commentary, as done in 01:59. [[User:QuaintCable|QuaintCable]] ([[User talk:QuaintCable|talk]])  <!--Template:Undated--><small class="autosigned">— Preceding [[Wikipedia:Signatures|undated]] comment added 02:46, 27 February 2026 (UTC)</small> <!--Autosigned by SineBot-->

Each image in an article should have a clear and unique illustrative purpose and serve as an important illustrative aid to understanding the subject.}} We don't use images as ''sources'' for [[WP:VERIFIABILITY]]; we use them to ''illustrate'' an article. The fact that the network of activists who posted the image protect their anonymity is not a problem, unless there's some doubt about whether the image is faked or [[AI slop]], but my impression is that AI slop typically gets refused by Commons.

To put things another way: we don't want [[WP:SPS]] as sources, but many photos on Commons that get used on Wikipedia ''are'' self-published by non-Wikipedia-notable people.

{{s|It would be good if we had more info about the image, but to me it seems reasonable to use it: it is a good illustration of the casualties (on a tiny scale).}}

content:

section: Concerns regarding Neutral Point of View (NPOV) and hagiographic tone
content:
{{Llmtop}}
There are several sections in this article that appear to deviate from a neutral, encyclopedic tone, leaning instead toward a hagiographic portrayal of the subject. To maintain compliance with WP:NPOV, the following elements should be reviewed and potentially rewritten for better balance and attribution:

Supernatural and Unverifiable Anecdotes: The inclusion of claims such as jumping from a moving train to perform prayers or the assertion that God puts words on his tongue during speeches reads more like religious biography than historical fact. These should be clearly attributed as claims made by the subject or his supporters, or removed if they lack historical weight.

Subjective and Promotional Labeling: Terms like "energy superpower" and descriptions of the subject as a "divine gift to mankind" are inherently non-neutral. Even when used by supporters, the frequency and weight given to these descriptors in the lead and key sections create a promotional bias.

Divine Lineage Claims: Presenting ancestry reaching back to a Shia Imam as a factual genealogical record, rather than a traditional or state-promoted claim, is inconsistent with encyclopedic standards for verifying ancient lineages.

Cultivated Imagery vs. Documented Wealth: The article places significant emphasis on a "spartan lifestyle" and pictures of gardening with a plastic watering can. While these are part of his public persona, they should be more directly contrasted with reports regarding his control over a US$95 billion financial empire to avoid reinforcing a specific political narrative.

Intellectual and Artistic Narrative: The portrayal of the subject as a "prodigy" who recalled excelling in studies or whose critiques of poetry were always affirmed by professionals relies heavily on personal or state-aligned reflections. These sections should be balanced with more objective analysis of his intellectual contributions and his role as a censor of the arts.

Synthesizing these points sugg...

Incorrect reference in _Legality of the Blockade_ section.

Hello, WikiFriends.

There is a long block quote citation in the ''Legality of the Blockade'' section that is incorrect.  The current citation incorrectly points to the "Protected Person Status in Occupied Iraq Under the Fourth Geneva Convention" OLC opinion.

Rather, the block quote came from Bisharat's ''Denver Journal of International Law & Policy'' article, which is cited in the next paragraph.  

Additionally, the next (non-quoted) paragraph is also directly from the article:

"...Israel instituted the blockade against the Gaza Strip not in
response to a violent attack, but rather in response to Hamas's ascension to exclusive authority in the Gaza Strip, and earlier in response to the Hamas victory in the 2006 Palestinian elections. Israel, in short, engaged in an act of war against an occupied people, and violated its legal obligations to them long before..."

Also, while Bisharat was a professor at UC Hastings when the Denver Journal article was published, I don't think it was an official finding of the University.  

Therefore, I suggest dropping the bracket-heavy block quote and just summarizing the relevant bits that refer to the Fourth Geneva Convention, as below.  

It also kind of gets lost in the block quote that Bisharat was talking about 2007's actions, rather than the current state of the blockade, so while many of these arguments are still live, if we're quoting Bisharat we should say which blockade he's talking about.

article: Environmental impact of the Gaza war
section: What should the scope of this article be?
content:
In the [[#Requested move 31 May 2024]] there was some discussion on what the scope of this article. From what I can see, we have three options:
*1. One article only – and no other articles – that covers the environmental impact across all theaters of the [[Israel-Hamas war]], including Gaza Strip, Israel, Lebanon, Red Sea etc.
*2. One article that gives the overview of the environmental impact of each theater of the war. Theaters with a lot of coverage (e.g Gaza Strip) would break into their own subarticles, while leaving a summary behind in the main article. This would be in accordance with [[Wikipedia:Summary style]].
*3. No overview article. Every theater's environmental impact is discussed in a separate article, with all the other theaters linked through the "See also" section.

Currently, it seems this article is #3. There is only content relating to Gaza Strip, and no other theaters. That's not necessarily a bad thing, but the title implies the scope of this article is either #1 or #2. One reason for asking is to determine where should we cover things like [https://apnews.com/article/red-sea-environment-cargo-ship-sinking-houthis-1e130e15ca0863ab40966ea9676cf42b oil spill caused by Houthi attacks], [https://www.barrons.com/news/oil-leaks-toxic-emissions-as-israel-strike-worsens-yemen-pollution-ngos-0f75df30 oil spill caused by IDF attacks], [https://www.reuters.com/world/middle-east/lebanese-farmers-dig-answers-israels-white-phosphorus-use-2024-07-03/ effect of white phosphorus on Lebanese agriculture], [https://www.jpost.com/environment-and-climate-change/article-783475 release of toxic material in Israel on October 7, 2023].

What do others think should be the scope of this article?'''[[User talk:Vice regent|VR]]''' <sub>(Please [[Template:Ping|ping]] on reply)</sub> 23:26, 4 August 2024 (UTC)

:This article seems the best home for the other information you mention. That would treat this as 1, although 1 and 2 are the same at the moment. This article is only...

The Africa Center for Strategic Studies, a think tank part of the U.S. Deptartment of Defense states the Houthis have ties with al-Shabaab, an al-Qaeda affiliate in Somalia [[Houthis#cite note-61|here]]. (the citation link has an extra l, which causes the 404 error)

Personally, I think this is highly questionable, ACSS bases their claim on a [https://docs.un.org/en/S/2025/71/Rev.1 2025 UN report], which in paragraph no. 40 states:

"Al-Shabaab was observed to have a developing relationship with the Houthis that was described as transactional or opportunistic, and not ideological. One Member State reported that Al-Shabaab held at least two meetings in Somalia with Houthi representatives in July and September, in which they requested advanced weapons and training."

Here are some important points:

- Only one unnamed member state made this claim

- Even the report describes the relationship as "transactional or opportunistic" and "not ideological", which weakens the case for real cooperation.

- A lack of evidence was presented in the source.

I think it's best to add "(alleged)" next to al-Shabaab in the non-state allies list per [[Wikipedia:Neutral point of view|WP:NPOV]]. The only source for the article's claim is a [https://africacenter.org/spotlight/al-shabaab-houthi-security-red-sea/ ACSS Spotlight article], citing a 2025 UN report citing one anonymous member state, and the relationship is described as “transactional or opportunistic.” [[User:Josemite|Josemite]] ([[User talk:Josemite|talk]]) 09:50, 1 August 2025 (UTC)

This is one of the most notable/read child articles of this topic, alongside the invasion article and casualties of the war that are both in the opening paragraph, thus it can easily be inserted into the sentence about Palestinian casualties. To clarify before it becomes a discussion, I don't see this as even an NPOV-related proposal. This is about providing navigation for the reader to the prominent subtopics of a [[WP:SUMMARY|higher-level summary article]] (as we've always done with this page); likewise it's about establishing the notability for the topic itself (which is largely based on the range of child articles and their popularity). To prove this, see the comparison views for equally prominently referenced child articles in past few months.[https://pageviews.wmcloud.org/?project=en.wikipedia.org&platform=all-access&agent=user&redirects=0&range=latest-90&pages=Gaza_war|Gaza_genocide|Casualties_of_the_Gaza_war|October_7_attacks] The genocide article is currently sought after more so than [[October 7 attacks]] and [[Casualties of the Gaza war]], and based on past year (including previous October 7 title), it was in between Casualties and October 7 for views (~1.5m, ~2m, and ~2.3m).[https://pageviews.wmcloud.org/...

'''Request removal or better sourcing for claim that Yoav Gallant ordered the Hannibal Directive to be implemented during the October 7th attacks.'''

I would request that the following passage is either removed or better sourced. The issues are as follows:

1. The first source given is from NDTV, but the link is broken.

2. The second is the Electronic Intifada, listed as an unreliable source here: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Wikipedia:Reliable_sources/Perennial_sources/3

3. The third is Middle East Monitor. The article isn't super clear on saying that he issued it (refers to "they") and is also listed here as being a source without consensus on its reliability: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Wikipedia:Reliable_sources/Perennial_sources/5

4. The fourth source is Anadolu Agency. The article doesn't say that Gallant ordered it, but in any case is listed as unreliable here for controversial and international topics: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Wikipedia:Reliable_sources/Perennial_sources/1#Anadolu_Agency_(controversial_topics)

I did try to find a better source for this but could not. Failing a better source being provided, I would request that this be removed.

section: RfC about ‘acceptance of the 1967 borders’: is our lead statement correct?
content:
* '''#A''': Reliable Source (RS) on topic [[Hamas]], Leila Seurat, when speaking (in her 2019 book, page 17) of Hamas’s “'''acceptance of the 1967 borders'''… [in] agreements… since 2005…” (and other RS with similar statements) '''mean to say that Hamas in such agreements has abandoned their ambition to create an Islamic state in all of former [[Mandatory Palestine]]'''.
That is why Wikipedia in the lead section of [[Hamas]] clearly asserts, referring to those quotes of Seurat, Baconi and Roy, that since 2005 Hamas has abandoned their quest for that larger state (“'''acquiescing to .. 2005 .. '''” as new Hamas position opposed to their “initially...” goal). Asserting in Wikipedia that Hamas since 2005 retains its long-term objective would then imply that Hamas’s acceptance of the 1967 borders in 2005 is invalidated or insincere; and attributing that false implication of insincerity to Leila Seurat (and other RS)—by suggesting that, according to Seurat (and others), Hamas retained that larger goal in 2005—would be misrepresenting Seurat (and others). (This is the explanation for the lead sentence “It began acquiescing…” as given by editor {{reply to|Smallangryplanet}} on 22Nov2025 and fully endorsed by {{reply to|Raskolnikov.Rev}} on 22 and 24 Nov2025.) * '''#B''': Reliable Source Seurat, in her book of 2019 on pages 14-16,{{harvnb|Seurat|2019|pp=14–17, 47}}: "[p.14] Since the early 1990s, Hamas began to formulate its foreign policy discourse in a way very different from the binary vision of the Charter. (…) At the heart of this new doctrine is a distinction between ‘short-term policy’ and the ‘long-term solution’. The former, often described as an ‘interim’ solution, was put forward for the first time in 1988 by [[Mahmoud al-Zahar]] (…) [p.15](…) The latter solution, also decribed as ‘historical’, emphasizes the sacred aspect of Palestine as a ''[[waqf]]'' (…) This dialectics between tactics (short term)...
